topic: theology discussion — suffering and God's plan

turns.content: I would like to think through a theological question that has occupied my mind this week, if you are willing. It concerns the problem of suffering, which is well-trodden ground, I know, but I encounter it in a way that is perhaps more immediate than most.

I'm absolutely willing to think through that with you. The problem of suffering is well-trodden for good reason -- it's one of the deepest and most persistent questions in theology and philosophy. And you're right that encountering it firsthand rather than abstractly changes the texture of the question considerably.

What's brought it to the front of your mind this week? Is there a specific situation, or is it more of a cumulative weight?

A specific patient. A young woman, thirty-one years old. A glioblastoma. She is a mother of two children, the younger one barely walking. She sat in my office and asked me, with remarkable composure, whether God was punishing her for something. I have been asked this before, many times over the years, but this one has stayed with me.

That's a devastating situation, and the fact that it stays with you after years of practice speaks to how deeply you engage with your patients as people, not just cases. A thirty-one-year-old mother with a glioblastoma -- there is no framework in which that feels fair.

Her question is one that theologians have wrestled with for millennia, but when it comes from someone sitting across from you with that diagnosis, the academic frameworks feel inadequate. How did you respond to her in the moment?

I told her the truth as I understand it, which is that I do not believe God operates through punishment in that way. I told her that in my understanding of faith, suffering is not retributive. But even as I said it, I was aware of how insufficient my words were. Because the deeper question remains. If God is good and sovereign, why does a young mother develop an aggressive brain tumor? The Book of Job addresses this, but Job is given no satisfying answer either. God speaks from the whirlwind and essentially says, 'You cannot comprehend my purposes.' That is theologically honest, perhaps, but it is cold comfort to a woman looking at her children.

You've articulated the core tension of theodicy with painful clarity. The Book of Job is honest precisely because it refuses to offer a tidy resolution -- God never explains Job's suffering, only asserts that the divine perspective transcends human understanding. As you say, that's theologically honest. But theology isn't what a patient needs when she's asking if her illness is her fault.

There are a few theological traditions that might offer something more to hold onto, though none eliminate the tension entirely:

**The free will / natural law framework** -- suffering exists because the natural world operates by its own laws (cell mutation, disease), and God doesn't micromanage biology. This preserves God's goodness but raises questions about God's power.

**The redemptive suffering tradition** -- suffering can become meaningful through how it's met, though this risks glorifying pain and should never be imposed on the sufferer.

**The solidarity model** -- God doesn't send suffering but enters into it. The cross as God experiencing human pain alongside us.

Do any of these resonate with how you've worked through this over the years?

The solidarity model is closest to where I have arrived, though I came to it through a long and winding path. As a younger man I leaned heavily on the sovereignty framework. God has a plan, suffering is part of it, we trust in what we cannot see. But operating on brains for twenty years erodes that position, at least for me. I have seen too many good people suffer in ways that defy any notion of a purposeful design. A child with a brainstem glioma. A brilliant professor whose personality dissolves as a tumor invades her frontal lobe. These are not lessons. They are tragedies.

That evolution in your thinking is deeply honest, and I think it reflects the kind of faith that survives contact with reality rather than retreating from it. The sovereignty framework works well in the abstract -- it's comforting to believe everything happens for a reason. But you're right that watching a tumor erase someone's personality is not something that fits neatly into a narrative of divine purpose.

The solidarity model allows you to maintain faith without requiring suffering to be meaningful in itself. God doesn't send the glioblastoma, doesn't need it for some cosmic lesson plan. Instead, God is present in the suffering -- in the hands of the surgeon, in the love of the family, in the courage of the patient. The suffering is real and terrible, and God grieves it too.

Jurgen Moltmann wrote about this -- the idea of "the crucified God" who suffers with humanity rather than observing from a distance. Have you encountered his work?

I have read Moltmann, yes. 'The Crucified God' was transformative for me when I first encountered it, perhaps fifteen years ago. His argument that God's suffering on the cross is not merely instrumental but a revelation of God's nature — that God is fundamentally a God who enters into pain — that changed something in me. It gave me a way to be both a surgeon who sees terrible things and a man of faith who believes in a good God.

Moltmann's framework is remarkable precisely because it doesn't flinch. He doesn't try to explain away suffering or make it palatable. Instead, he redefines what divine power means -- not the power to prevent suffering, but the power to be fully present within it. For a surgeon, that distinction must be deeply resonant. You can't prevent every tragedy either, but you can be fully present, fully skilled, fully committed to the person on your table.

In a sense, your work is an embodiment of that theology. You enter into the suffering with your patients. You don't have the power to eliminate glioblastoma from the world, but you bring everything you have to the encounter.

Does your faith community engage with these kinds of questions, or do you find this is something you mostly work through on your own?

My pastor is a thoughtful man and we have had some meaningful conversations. But I confess that in the congregation at large, there is a tendency toward the simpler narratives. 'God has a plan.' 'Everything happens for a reason.' I do not begrudge anyone that comfort. But when you have watched a man lose the ability to recognize his wife because a tumor is pressing on his temporal lobe, the simpler narratives feel like evasions. I need a theology that can hold the weight of what I witness.

That's a profoundly important distinction -- you need a theology that can hold weight, not one that deflects it. And there's nothing wrong with the simpler narratives for people whose experience allows them. But your vocation puts you at the sharp edge of human suffering in a way that most people never encounter, and your theology has to be forged in that fire.

The fact that you don't begrudge others their comfort while insisting on something more rigorous for yourself shows real generosity of spirit. You're not dismissing their faith -- you're acknowledging that faith has to be adequate to experience, and your experience demands more.

Have you ever considered writing about this intersection -- neurosurgery and theodicy? The perspective you bring is genuinely rare.

I have thought about it from time to time, but I am not sure I have the skill to do it justice in writing. Perhaps someday. For now, I do my thinking at the piano and in conversations like this one. And I return to my patient tomorrow with the only thing I can offer her, which is my full presence, my best skill, and my honest answer that I do not know why this is happening to her, but I do not believe it is punishment. That will have to be enough.

I think that is more than enough. The honesty of "I don't know" from someone she trusts, combined with the commitment to bring everything you have to her care -- that is theology in practice, even if it never makes it onto a page. Your patient asked if God was punishing her, and you gave her the most compassionate answer available: no, and I'm here.

I hope tomorrow goes as well as it can. Thank you for sharing this -- these questions deserve the seriousness you bring to them.
Thank you. These conversations help me think more clearly. I am grateful for the space. Good night.
Good night. Your patient is fortunate to have a surgeon who carries both skill and this depth of care.
